payload.text:
1) High-level feature list (what the app must do)

Home screen with:

Upload / Generate MCQ button

Summarize button (beside History)

History section showing saved quizzes and saved summaries (two separate tabs/lists).

PDF / Document upload (accepts PDF, DOCX, TXT, PPTX, images). If scanned images, run OCR.

MCQ generator:

User clicks Generate → app extracts only content-relevant questions (no unrelated questions).

For each generated MCQ: question text, 4 options (one correct), and explanation (hidden until after submit? — but you asked explanation shown beneath each question after submit).

Allow user to select one option per question.

A Submit button at the end: after submit, show score and mark answers:

If user selected correct option → that selected option highlighted green.

If user selected wrong option → selected option highlighted red, and the actual correct option highlighted green.

Beneath every question show a clear explanation text (explanation visible after submit).

Download button (bottom-right) to download the generated quiz as a PDF.

Save button (bottom-left) to save that quiz into History.

Summarizer:

Accessible from Home beside History.

Upload any file → produce a summary covering all important topics (concise, bullet/section format).

Show a Download button (left) and Save button (right) in the summary page.

Saved summaries are stored in History (separate list from saved MCQs).

History page:

Shows saved MCQs and Saved Summaries in separate tabs.

Allow open/view, re-download, and delete saved items.

Additional UX:

Progress indicator while generating questions/summaries.

Option to choose number of MCQs or difficulty (optional).

Allow re-generating / regenerating with different parameters.

Privacy/Export:

Local user save (per-account) or guest storage (localStorage) depending on auth choice.

Export single quiz as PDF (print-friendly formatting).

2) Suggested tech stack (practical & common)

Frontend: React (create-react-app or Vite) + Tailwind CSS for quick UI (you asked earlier for Tailwind-style in other projects). UI components: simple cards, buttons, modal dialogs.

Backend: FastAPI (Python) or Node.js/Express — I recommend Python if you’ll use NLP libraries. Use REST endpoints for file upload / generate / save / history.

Storage:

For a simple app: SQLite or JSON files on server.

For production or multiple users: Postgres + file storage (S3 or server disk).

NLP & PDF processing (recommended libs listed below).

LLM/question generation:

Option A: Use a cloud LLM API (OpenAI, Google Gemini, etc.) to generate questions and explanations.

Option B (self-hosted): use an on-prem model or open LLM (if cost/privacy is required).

PDF generation for download: jsPDF (frontend) or WeasyPrint / wkhtmltopdf (backend).

OCR for scanned docs: Tesseract OCR.

Optional: Streamlit for a quick prototype (there are open-source Streamlit MCQ generator projects).

3) Libraries & tools (evidence-based recommendations)

Text extraction: pdfplumber or PyMuPDF (MuPDF / fitz) — both widely used for extracting text and tables from PDFs. PyMuPDF is noted by many as very effective; pdfplumber is great for layout-sensitive extraction. 
Stack Overflow
+1

Existing open-source MCQ projects you can reuse or study: mcqgenerator repos on GitHub (Streamlit + OpenAI), and multiple NLP MCQ projects — good for reference. 
GitHub
+1

AI quiz & summarizer services (examples of features you can match): PDFQuiz, Mindgrasp, Questgen, Smallpdf's summarizer—these show typical features and UX to follow. 
Smallpdf
+3
PDFQuiz
+3
Mindgrasp
+3

Summarization examples / tools: Many small AI summarizers and open GitHub summarizers exist (use LLM-based chunking + summarization). 
GitHub
+1

4) MCQ generation algorithm (concrete steps you should implement)

Extract text from PDF:

Use PyMuPDF/pdfplumber. If file type is not PDF, convert or extract using appropriate library (python-docx for DOCX).

If scanned pdf / images, run OCR (Tesseract) before extraction. 
Stack Overflow

Preprocess & chunk:

Clean text; split into logical chunks (chapters, headings, paragraphs) by length (e.g., 800–1500 tokens per chunk) to stay within LLM limits.

Detect headings and create a TOC if possible so questions are tied to specific sections.

Identify candidate facts:

Use heuristics or an NLP pipeline (sentence scoring: named entities, dates, numbers, definitions, bold/italic text, headings) to pick statements suitable for MCQs (facts, definitions, relationships).

Question generation per fact:

For each selected fact, prompt an LLM to create:

a clear question

one correct answer (extracted/derived from text)

3 plausible distractors (incorrect options) — distractors should be plausible and derived from text or generated semantically similar to the correct answer.

a short explanation (1–3 sentences) citing the source text (or paraphrasing).

Prompt engineering tips: include context chunk & the exact sentence; ask the model to only use content from the chunk.

Quality control:

Check duplicates, remove ambiguous questions, ensure distractors aren’t accidentally correct (validate by searching the PDF content).

Optionally run a short “sanity-check” verification prompt to the LLM: “Is the correct answer unique? Are any two options identical?”

UI ordering & user preview:

Present the questions in the UI grouped by section. Allow user to request more/less questions or regenerate particular question.

5) Summarization algorithm (practical)

Chunk the document using heading-aware chunker.

Use LLM to summarize each chunk, then combine chunk summaries and condense into a final summary covering key topics (bulleted sections).

Include “Key topics” list and optionally time stamps/locations in the doc where each topic appears.

Offer both short (3–6 bullet) and detailed (per-section) summaries.

6) UI / UX specifics to match your color & placement requirements

Quiz screen layout:

Each question card shows question text + radio list of 4 options.

Explanation text is hidden until after Submit (or you can show it collapsed and expand on submit).

At the bottom: left corner Save button; right corner Download (PDF) button; center Submit button.

After Submit:

Score displayed at top (e.g., “Score: 7/10 — 70%”).

For each question:

If user selected correct option → highlight that option green.

If selected wrong option → highlight selected option red and the correct option green.

Beneath the question, show the explanation text (visible and clear).

History page:

Two tabs: Saved Quizzes and Saved Summaries.

Each saved item shows: title (auto-derived from PDF + date), preview, “Open / Download / Delete” actions.

Download PDF:

Clicking Download triggers styled PDF generation (print-friendly): questions, the options, correct answer flagged (or leave answers hidden if user prefers), and explanations (as you specified to show answers below each question).

Accessibility: ensure color contrast; also show icons (tick/cross) along with green/red for color-blind accessibility.

7) Data model & API endpoints (backend)

Example DB tables:

users (optional)

files (id, user_id, filename, filepath, uploaded_at, metadata)

quizzes (id, user_id, title, source_file_id, questions_json, generated_at, num_questions)

summaries (id, user_id, title, source_file_id, summary_text, generated_at)

Example endpoints (REST):

POST /upload — accept file, return file_id.

POST /generate/mcq — body: {file_id, num_questions, difficulty?} → returns quiz object.

POST /generate/summary — body: {file_id, length?} → returns summary object.

POST /quizzes/:id/save — save a generated quiz to history.

GET /history?type=mcq|summary — list saved items.

GET /quizzes/:id/download — return PDF file.

DELETE /history/:id — delete saved item.

8) Security, privacy & scale considerations

If you send PDF content to third-party LLMs, ensure users know their documents are sent off-server (privacy).

Option to self-host models or limit sensitive docs (local-only mode).

Rate limits to avoid heavy LLM usage from single user.

If you plan to store user files, consider encryption at rest or expiry policies.

9) Implementation + useful references (what I searched & found)

Open-source MCQ examples (use as code references / prototypes): mcqgenerator on GitHub (Streamlit + OpenAI) — good starter. 
GitHub

Text extraction advice: pdfplumber and PyMuPDF are solid choices for extracting text from PDFs; use OCR for scanned docs. 
Stack Overflow
+1

Existing AI quiz & summarizer services (for UX ideas): PDFQuiz, Mindgrasp, Smallpdf’s summarizer and Questgen — examine how they show options, download flow and history. 
Questgen - AI Quiz Generator
+3
PDFQuiz
+3
Mindgrasp
+3

10) Deliverables checklist (what I will hand over / you should expect)

Full project specification (this document).

UI mockups (Home, Upload, Quiz screen, Summarize screen, History).

Backend API spec (endpoints + sample request/response).

Data model (SQL schema).

Example prompt templates for LLM question generation & distractor generation.

Recommended libraries list + install & run notes.

A minimal prototype plan (stack: React + FastAPI + SQLite + pdfplumber + OpenAI).

Testing checklist: unit tests for extraction, integration tests for generation, manual QA for color/feedback rules.

Deployment notes (Heroku / Vercel + server for LLM calls).

11) Example LLM prompt templates (useful)

Generate MCQ (single fact)

Context: <pass the chunk or the sentence extracted from PDF here>

Task: Create one clear multiple-choice question based only on the context above. Provide:
- question: a single concise sentence.
- options: 4 options labeled A, B, C, D.
- correct_option: letter
- explanation: 1-2 sentences explaining why the correct option is right, referencing the context.

Constraints:
- Do not invent facts not in the context.
- Distractors must be plausible and not directly stated as true in the context.


Summarize chunk

Context: <chunk text>

Task: Produce a short summary (3-6 bullet points) that captures the important topics in the context. Keep sentences simple and cite any headings if present.

12) UX details exactly matching your color/placement instructions

Buttons at bottom: Save at left bottom; Download at bottom-right. Ensure mobile responsiveness (sticky bottom bar).

After quiz submit: selected correct → green; selected wrong → red; correct option → green. Explanation text shown below each question.

History view shows separate sections for MCQ Saved and Summaries Saved (tabs or left-side filter).

When saving, store: title (derived from PDF name + “— Quiz” or “— Summary”), date/time, preview.

13) Next steps for you (if you want me to continue)

I can do any of these next actions (pick one):

Provide fully detailed UI mockups (HTML/CSS/React component code) for the main screens.

Provide backend sample code (FastAPI + example endpoints + simple MCQ generator using OpenAI).

Provide a minimal working prototype in Streamlit (faster demo) that accepts PDF, extracts text, calls an LLM for MCQs, and shows results.

(If you tell me which of the above you want right now, I will produce it immediately — code, mocks, or a prototype. No waiting required.)

Key web sources I used while preparing this plan

PDF quiz & AI quiz services (examples for features and UX): PDFQuiz, Mindgrasp, AceQuiz, Questgen. 
Questgen - AI Quiz Generator
+3
PDFQuiz
+3
Mindgrasp
+3

PDF text extraction & library discussions (pdfplumber, PyMuPDF recommended). 
Stack Overflow
+1

Open-source MCQ generator GitHub examples (good prototypes to adapt). 
GitHub
+1

Summarizer tools & projects (Smallpdf summarizer and GitHub summarizers).

**Defining Application Scope**

I've been solidifying the scope of the React application. Currently, I'm focusing on the core functionalities: file upload capabilities, parsing different file formats (PDF, DOCX, TXT, PPTX, and images), and then generating MCQs and summaries based on the uploaded content. My aim is to make it a seamless SPA experience with React 18+, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS.


**Refining Architecture Choices**

I'm now contemplating how to integrate the Gemini API for content generation. The frontend will likely handle this directly, calling Gemini. I'll need to mock it if a full backend isn't feasible. The homepage will feature upload/generate buttons for MCQs and summaries with a history section displaying saved quizzes and summaries. Text extraction from uploaded files is crucial, and since a backend isn't specified, I'll focus on browser-based text extraction.


**Analyzing File Processing Strategies**

I'm now zeroing in on the file processing logic. While libraries like `pdfjs-dist` handle PDF extraction, DOCX and PPTX are more challenging in a frontend-only setup. I'm leaning towards a strategy that uses Gemini's multimodal capabilities. The plan is to convert PDF pages to images for processing, and for text files, direct text input will be considered. I'll utilize the `@google/genai` SDK and explore sending file bytes directly if it is supported.


**Devising Frontend Text Handling**

I've been thinking about the practicalities of text extraction and file handling in this frontend-centric approach. While initially considering a full text extraction simulation, I'm now leaning towards simpler strategies. For text files and markdown, I'll read the content directly.  Images will be base64 encoded and sent to Gemini. PDFs present a challenge. To meet the user request, I'll design a user interface that implies PDF handling.  For the API call, I might mock the extraction step or use placeholder text, focusing on the core MCQ and summarization workflow.


**Enhancing File Extraction**

I'm now integrating PDF text extraction directly into the frontend. I'm leveraging `pdfjs-dist` through a CDN, aiming for a truly functional app. This direct extraction is a pivotal development, addressing a key challenge. I'm streamlining the PDF handling process and making it more robust.


**Mapping Architectural Details**

I'm now concretizing the application's component structure and integrating the Gemini API. The current plan involves using `jspdf` for PDF generation, the `@google/genai` library for all API interactions, and potentially `react-markdown` for markdown rendering in summaries, if needed. The frontend architecture is taking shape: a router in `App.tsx` controls the views, a `Layout.tsx` component provides the common structure, and specific components handle file uploads, MCQ generation, summaries, and history. I'll utilize the `gemini-2.5-flash` model for optimal performance/cost, and plan to use the `responseSchema` feature for structured JSON output when generating MCQs.


**Improving Gemini Integration**

I'm now focusing on integrating the Gemini API more effectively, especially with the `systemInstruction`. This addition will ensure that the generated MCQs and summaries are relevant to the uploaded content. I'm also planning to implement a consistent styling, making use of Tailwind's indigo/blue as the primary color and employing green, red, and slate for status messages. I've broken down the implementation plan into clear steps: setting up the base files, building necessary services (Gemini, storage, PDF generation), constructing reusable UI components, and connecting the UI to the Gemini service.


**Defining Local Storage Structure**

I'm solidifying the application's local storage strategy for maintaining the user history. I'll need to define the exact structure of the history objects, considering `id`, `type` (MCQ or summary), `date`, `title`, and `content` fields. I'll also ensure robust error handling and data validation for the local storage interactions.

**Optimizing PDF Handling**

I'm solidifying PDF handling and considering potential complexities. While `pdfjs-dist` is now standard, I'm making it robust, with an API. For downloads, `jspdf` will be utilized. I'm also now structuring the project for efficiency. I'm reorganizing files for a more streamlined structure, for a handful of files, as requested. I've chosen `gemini-2.5-flash` model with `application/json` output for MCQs, and I'm feeling confident in the progress.


**Defining Content Generation Strategy**

I'm presently exploring the most efficient method for generating content within the constraints of this project. While direct Gemini calls are essential, I'm now contemplating the most effective methods. Although I don't see any hardware permissions needed, I've confirmed that the 'file' input is standard, and there's no camera or microphone requirement. The approach is simple: focusing on upload features as primary functionality. I'll make a decision and then build the files.

payload.text:
#1 Voice-Based Quiz Reader & Voice Answers (Feature 4)

What & why

Read quiz content aloud and let users answer by voice. Great for accessibility, mobile learners, and audio-first studying.

UX

Play / Pause / Next / Previous controls.

“Read current question only” vs “Read entire quiz.”

Mic button to answer aloud (with visual confirmation).

Transcript & confidence indicator shown after each voice answer.

Option to repeat question or confirm selected option.

Implementation

Text-to-Speech (TTS): use browser Web Speech API (SpeechSynthesis) for quick support, or cloud TTS (Google Cloud TTS, AWS Polly) for better voices and languages.

Speech-to-Text (STT): Web Speech API (SpeechRecognition) for browsers OR cloud STT (Whisper, Google Speech-to-Text) for higher accuracy and multi-language support.

Flow: TTS reads question → enable STT listener for X seconds → parse returned text to nearest option using similarity (edit distance / semantically via embeddings). Confirm match with user if low confidence.

Algorithms & details

Convert recognized text to normalized label: user may say “Option B” or “two” or spoken answer text.

Use fuzzy matching: map recognized phrase to option labels by:

Check for explicit labels (A/B/C/D, 1/2/one/two).

If not found, compute semantic similarity between recognized phrase and option texts (use embeddings + cosine similarity). If highest similarity > threshold → accept. Else ask for confirmation.

Save voice audio if consented (for training / analytics).

Edge cases & quality checks

Noisy environments: show a “low confidence” state and prompt for confirmation.

Language mismatches: require language setting or detect automatically.

Privacy: request permission and explain whether audio is uploaded to server.

Complexity: Medium

**Envisioning Voice Interaction**

I'm now firmly focusing on integrating voice interaction.  The design process involves defining the optimal flow for a seamless experience. My thoughts center around implementing a TTS system to announce questions and options, paired with an STT system to understand user responses.  The priority now is to explore the best STT engines for accuracy.


**Defining User Controls**

I've just added several new UI controls to the `QuizGenerator.tsx` component. During the quiz taking phase, users now have a play/pause, microphone, and read controls to manage their experience. I'm exploring the tech stack implications too.  For TTS, I'm leaning toward using Gemini 2.5 Flash Native Audio via the Live API when feasible, or the Web Speech API for certain interactions.  The instructions guide me to consider `gemini-2.5-flash-preview-tts` too.


**Optimizing Readability Implementation**

Okay, I've just solidified my approach to the "Read Aloud" functionality. After careful consideration, and specifically reviewing the user instructions, I've decided to leverage the browser's `window.speechSynthesis` API for the "Read Question" feature. This option aligns perfectly with the requirement for quick support and low latency, essential for a seamless quiz experience. This reduces API usage and keeps user interaction fast.


**Implementing Voice Controls**

Now I'm diving deeper into the specifics of voice interaction. My focus is on the "Mic" button, aiming for a smooth "answer aloud" feature. I'm actively crafting the states for listening, transcription, and confidence levels. For UI components, I'll integrate the speaker icon to trigger question reading, and the mic icon to initiate voice input. The plan involves utilizing `window.SpeechRecognition` for STT, to capture answers.


**Defining Answer Matching**

I'm now focusing on the logic for matching the speech transcript to the multiple-choice options. I've mapped out how to handle direct options like "A" or "Option B", and plan to use Levenshtein distance or string inclusion for more complex matches. The microphone permission is added too. I've also outlined refinements in TTS and STT interactions: cancelling speech, reading questions, and options, showing the mic indicator and processing the answer. I'll make a helper to keep things tidy.


**Integrating Microphone Input**

I've just focused on integrating the microphone input. I've updated the `QuizGenerator.tsx` component with several new UI elements, including buttons for "Read Question" and "Answer by Voice," along with the associated state and logic. The state for `isListening` and `voiceTranscript` are now integrated. I've also implemented the functions `speakText`, `stopSpeaking`, `startListening`, and `processVoiceAnswer`. The matching logic now accounts for direct options by starting with "a", "b", "c", or "d". Finally, the `metadata.json` has also been updated to include the necessary `microphone` permission.


**Enhancing UI for Voice Input**

I'm now refining the UI for voice interaction, aiming for a more intuitive user experience. I've decided to place the voice controls within the "Voice Assistant" section, specifically inside the `taking` step.  The plan involves integrating the "Read Question" and "Answer by Voice" buttons near the question text for easy access. The challenge now lies in ensuring broad browser compatibility, as `SpeechRecognition`'s availability varies. I'll employ feature detection to gracefully handle any browser-specific inconsistencies and I need to add that to the metadata.json.


**Adding Interactive Voice Features**

I'm currently working to integrate interactive voice functionalities into the quiz. The goal is to allow users to both hear the questions and answer using their voice. I'm focusing on the implementation of the speakText and stopSpeaking functions for the user, managing the `isSpeaking` and `isListening` states. I'm exploring the nuances of `speechSynthesis` and plan to use an icon for visual feedback.


**Adding Speech Recognition**

I've just focused on implementing the voice input using `SpeechRecognition`. I've added error handling and a "Listening..." state. The code now transcribes speech, sets a transcript, matches the voice answer, and stops listening. I've also incorporated error handling.


**Refining Matching Accuracy**

I'm now refining the matching logic to improve accuracy. I've decided to normalize user input by lowercasing and trimming whitespace, which will help with inconsistencies. My plan involves checking for direct option matches like "A" or "Option B", and then using substring matching for more complex responses. Levenshtein distance seems excessive for this, so I will prioritize simpler methods. I'll test these matching methods on the user interface.


**Integrating Voice Interaction**

payload.text:
#2 Plagiarism Detector in Summary Mode (Feature 6)

What & why

Detect whether the generated summary is too similar to public sources or the source document (useful for academic integrity and verifying originality).

UX

After summary, show a “Similarity score: 12%” and a small list of flagged lines with sources (if available). Option to “Regenerate” with lower similarity.

Implementation

Approach A (local): Use near-duplicate detection against the source_doc: split summary into sentences, compute overlap with source text (n-gram Jaccard) — reports when summary copies exact lines (plagiarism within same doc).

Approach B (web check): For web-based similarity, use third-party plagiarism APIs (Turnitin, Copyscape) or implement search-engine-based heuristics (query search engine with quoted substrings) — note legal/ToS issues.

Approach C (embedding-based): Index web / allowed corpora with embeddings and check cosine similarity for sentences; a high similarity signals potential copying.

Algorithms & tools

Sentence-level comparison: compute normalized Levenshtein or n-gram overlap to detect verbatim copying.

Semantic overlap: use sentence embeddings (OpenAI or other) to detect paraphrase-based plagiarism.

Edge cases

Summarizer may need to quote exact lines (e.g., definitions) — allow “allowed quotes” threshold.

False positives for common phrases; surface evidence with exact lines flagged.

Complexity: High (web checks) / Medium (local checks)

**Implementing Plagiarism Detection**

I'm currently working on adding a Plagiarism Detector to the Summary Mode.  Initial focus is on a local similarity check to flag highly similar lines post-summarization. My next step will be integrating the similarity score display and exploring the optional web check capability.


**Analyzing Summary Originality**

I'm now prioritizing local similarity with the source document as the primary plagiarism check. Given the React and Gemini architecture, this is the most direct and useful. I'm also considering integrating web search grounding via Gemini's `googleSearch` tool for an additional layer of originality detection, though this is secondary. The key is distinguishing between extractive and abstractive summarization.


**Focusing Similarity Calculation**

I'm now prioritizing the algorithmic similarity check within the front-end for accurate local comparison, calculating a similarity score and identifying flagged segments. I've updated the plan to refine the process: I will adjust `types.ts` to include `similarityScore` and `flaggedSegments`. Next, I'll build a utility function to compute this score and highlight similar sentences within `Summarizer.tsx`, making the web check an optional future enhancement, prioritizing the direct comparison first.


**Integrating Web Originality Check**

I've revised the plan to incorporate a more robust approach to detecting potential plagiarism. My focus is now on a hybrid system. First, I'll calculate the n-gram overlap to identify exact copies from the source document. Secondly, I'll use Gemini's search tool for a "Web Originality Check," essentially asking it to determine if the summary appears verbatim online. This two-pronged strategy should effectively address both local similarity and broader web plagiarism concerns, balancing cost-effectiveness with functionality.


**Improving Plagiarism Feedback**

I'm now integrating a plagiarism analysis structure within the `Summary` interface. This involves defining a `PlagiarismResult` with the score and detected matches, as well as a more direct `checkWebPlagiarism` method using Gemini with search grounding. A key adjustment is my simplified "Web Plagiarism" logic: rather than relying on parsing, I'll now instruct Gemini to rewrite if it finds similar web content, or at least identify the matches. This focuses the process to ensure originality and reduce costs.


**Optimizing Similarity Checks**

I'm now prioritizing the local similarity check, leveraging a dedicated `analysisService.ts`. This involves calculating the score and identifying matches within the `Summarizer.tsx` view. I'll display the "Source Similarity" score and highlight flagged segments immediately after summarization.  A "Check Web Originality" button will also initiate a Gemini-powered web search for further validation. This approach emphasizes quick local feedback and provides an on-demand web check for deeper analysis.


**Implementing a Two-Tiered Check**

I'm now focusing on a two-pronged plagiarism detection method: a direct local comparison of source and summary text, and an optional web check using Gemini. I will calculate a "Source Similarity" score using a sentence-level check. For each sentence, I will search for near matches in the original source, flagging those found. The similarity score will then be calculated as the percentage of characters in flagged sentences compared to the total summary characters. Following this local check, I'll then integrate a web check with Gemini to search for any exact matches. I will use the `googleSearch` tool and instruct the model to report any exact matches found. I plan to implement these changes across `types.ts`, `services/analysisService.ts`, and `components/Summarizer.tsx`.


**Implementing Enhanced Analysis**

I'm now focusing on integrating `checkWebPlagiarism` into `geminiService.ts` using `googleSearch` to determine if summary content exists online.  I've updated the `types.ts` file to include `PlagiarismMatch` and `Summary` interfaces that include plagiarism analysis data. I'm also planning UI updates in `Summarizer.tsx` to display the plagiarism score. I need to make sure the extracted text is available for comparison. I'm planning to use the existing `extractTextFromFile` to do this.


**Enhancing Similarity Logic**

I'm currently working to refine the local similarity check, implementing sentence-level comparisons within `analyzeLocalSimilarity`.  I'll compare sentences from the summary against the source, identifying exact matches and calculating a similarity score based on the percentage of matching characters. This function filters out short sentences for better accuracy. Finally, I'll return the score and list of matches for display.


**Developing Web Check Integration**

I'm now integrating the web originality check within the `Summarizer` UI. I'll include a button to initiate the web check via Gemini, using the `googleSearch` tool and instructing it to identify any sentences appearing verbatim online. The "Similarity Score (Local)" will be displayed, and matching sections will be highlighted.


**Adding Local and Web Checks**

I'm now integrating both the local and web plagiarism checks. `analysisService.ts` will handle the shingle-based local logic, while `geminiService.ts` will manage the web check. The `Summarizer.tsx` view will display both. The local check will use a shingle approach for scoring, but highlight entire sentences that are present in the source. Gemini will perform the web check, identifying sentences found verbatim online. I'll need to create the `analysisService.ts` file, and I'll update the prompt to instruct Gemini to return sentences that are verbatim online.


**Finalizing Plagiarism Integration**

I'm now integrating the local and web plagiarism checks. The `analysisService.ts` now handles local shingle-based logic, while `geminiService.ts` manages the web check. The `Summarizer.tsx` view will display both. The local check identifies sentences present in the source. Gemini performs the web check, finding verbatim online sentences using `googleSearch`. I need to create the `analysisService.ts` and will update the prompt to instruct Gemini to identify verbatim online matches. I also need to make sure the extracted text from the source is passed to the analysis service, as the existing `fileService.ts` function can provide it.


**Adding Web Score Calculation**

I'm now integrating the web originality analysis by leveraging Gemini's `googleSearch` tool. I'll instruct the model to locate the source of any verbatim sentences. The `groundingMetadata` in the response will provide the URLs. Then, I'll calculate a "Web Score" based on the results. I'm also planning to move local analysis directly into `analysisService.ts` for better organization and simplicity. Furthermore, I'll allow a "Regenerate with lower similarity" option by instructing the model to rewrite with more abstract language.

I will implement the Plagiarism Detector feature for the Summarizer mode.

This includes:
1.  **Local Similarity Check**: Compares the generated summary against the uploaded document to detect copy-pasting (extractive vs. abstractive).
2.  **Web Originality Check**: Uses Gemini's Google Search Grounding to check if parts of the summary appear verbatim on the web.
3.  **UI Updates**: Displays a similarity score, highlights flagged sentences in the text, and offers a "Regenerate (More Original)" option.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Update `Summary` to include plagiarism analysis data.
2.  **`services/analysisService.ts`**: Create a new service for local similarity calculation (n-gram/sentence overlap).
3.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Add `checkWebOriginality` to use Gemini with Search tools, and `regenerateSummary` for re-writing content.
4.  **`views/Summarizer.tsx`**: Update the UI to show the score, highlighting, and controls.

Added `checkWebOriginality` (using Search) and `regenerateSummary` (rewriting) functions.

Integrated plagiarism detection (local & web), highlighting of similar text, and regeneration flow.

#3 Explanation Depth Selector (Feature 7)

What & why

Let users choose how detailed the explanation under each MCQ should be: Simple / Detailed / Step-by-step / Story.

UX

Toggle before generation: “Explanation depth: Simple / Detailed / Step-by-step / Story.”

Preview one question in each mode.

Implementation

Use LLM prompts with a depth parameter:

Simple: 1-2 sentences, concise reasoning.

Detailed: 3–6 sentences, with bullet points and short examples.

Step-by-step: If reasoning or multi-step (math), show stepwise derivation.

Story: short scenario or mnemonic tying the fact to memory.

Maintain token budget (shorter for Simple; larger for Detailed).

Prompt examples

Context: <relevant sentence>
Task: Generate an explanation for the correct option.
Depth: detailed
Output: 3-5 short sentences, mention the key phrase from the context.


Edge cases

For highly technical questions, "story" may be inappropriate—fall back to detailed.

Complexity: Low → Medium

**Defining Explanation Depth**

I'm currently focused on defining the "Explanation Depth" levels.  Should the options be "Simple," "Detailed," and maybe "Advanced," or are different labels more intuitive? I'm exploring how to translate these abstract levels into concrete content guidelines for the quiz generator.  This will drive the UI decisions.


**Incorporating Depth Parameter**

I'm now integrating the `explanationDepth` parameter into the quiz generation flow. I'm adding a UI control for it in `QuizGenerator.tsx` and updating `generateQuizFromText` in `geminiService.ts` to accept the new parameter. Crucially, I'm modifying the prompt within `geminiService.ts` to instruct the LLM on how to generate explanations based on the selected depth level. I'm also considering storing the depth level metadata in the `Quiz` interface.


**Aligning Quiz Data**

I've added the `explanationDepth` property to the `Quiz` interface in `types.ts`, aligning with the existing structure. Now, `generateQuizFromText` in `geminiService.ts` accepts and uses this parameter. The prompt now has specific instructions, varying explanations for Simple, Detailed, Step-by-step, or Story modes. UI updates will follow, including a selector and display of this depth.


**Implementing Explanation Styles**

Now I'm focusing on defining different explanation styles within the LLM prompt. I've added `"Step-by-step"` and `"Story"` options to the `explanationDepth` parameter. I'm adding specific instructions to the prompt to control how the LLM generates explanations, like breaking down reasoning into steps or creating short scenarios for the "Story" mode. Next up is UI integration and data handling.


**Updating LLM Prompts**

I've just modified the prompt in `geminiService.ts` to include the `explanationDepth` and `selectedInstruction` dynamically.  I'm focusing on incorporating user-selected styles into the prompt, specifying clear instructions for the LLM to provide explanations tailored to `Simple`, `Detailed`, or specific styles like `Step-by-step` and `Story`. The UI changes in `QuizGenerator.tsx` now include a dropdown to select the explanation depth.


**Adding Depth Selection UI**

I've incorporated the UI elements, including a dropdown selector for explanation depth in `QuizGenerator.tsx`.  The selected value is passed to the quiz generation function, and I'm updating the `Quiz` interface to store the `explanationDepth`.  I'm also preparing to display this depth setting in the results header, making it clear to the user. Next, I will test this UI.

I will add the **Explanation Depth Selector** feature, allowing users to customize how detailed the answer explanations are (Simple, Detailed, Step-by-step, or Story).

Here are the changes:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Add `explanationDepth` to the `Quiz` interface.
2.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Update `generateQuizFromText` to accept the `explanationDepth` parameter and modify the prompt to strictly enforce the selected explanation style.
3.  **`views/QuizGenerator.tsx`**: Add the dropdown selector in the configuration panel and pass the selected value to the generation service.

Added `explanationDepth` to Quiz interface.

#5 Auto-Topic Detection & Chapter-wise Quizzes (Feature 10)

What & why

Automatically split the PDF into chapters/sections and let users generate quizzes per chapter or the whole book.

UX

Show a generated Table of Contents (TOC) with checkboxes per chapter.

Buttons: “Generate chapter quiz” or “Generate full quiz.”

Implementation

Heading detection: detect headings by analyzing PDF text sizes/positions (pdfplumber or PyMuPDF provides font-size and layout metadata). For plain text files, detect headings via numbered lines (1., Chapter, Section, etc.).

Chunking: once TOC is built, extract corresponding text ranges.

User selection: pass only selected chunks to MCQ generator.

Algorithms

Use layout-aware extraction to capture heading locations and associate following paragraphs.

If TOC not present, use topic modeling (LDA / embeddings + clustering) to group text into “topics/chapters” automatically.

Edge cases

Scanned PDFs without clear layout: run OCR + try heuristics on line patterns.

Multi-column layouts require careful text ordering.

Complexity: Medium

**Considering Automatic Chapters**

I'm currently focused on how to automatically detect chapters within uploaded documents. I'm exploring different methods for parsing the text and identifying structural cues that might indicate chapter boundaries. Displaying these to users with checkboxes seems feasible, and then I'm thinking of creating chapter-based quizzes.


**Exploring TOC Generation**

I've been looking into how to make quiz creation more flexible. I'm thinking I can let the user pick specific chapters for the quiz to be based on. I've updated the plan to leverage Gemini for generating a Table of Contents from the extracted text, rather than relying on tricky PDF font size checks. Currently, I'm working on the data structure for chapter/topic information.


**Implementing Gemini Integration**

My latest thought is to integrate Gemini for document structure analysis. I'm focusing on a new `analyzeDocumentStructure` function within `geminiService.ts`, aiming to identify chapters and sections in text. The prompt will request a structured JSON output of topics, potentially streamlining the chapter detection process.

I'm now refining the topic extraction process for quiz generation. Initially, I planned for descriptions, but realized we need the *content* for quiz creation. My thinking is to pass `selectedTopics` and the `fullText` to the quiz generator. The updated prompt will now use a focused topic list. I'm moving away from complex text slicing on the front end, making this a simpler backend process.

I'm now thinking about how to improve the user experience and make the quiz generation process smoother. I'm focusing on incorporating a topic selection step after document analysis. The goal is to let users choose specific topics or default to the entire document. I'm currently working on the UI flow, including showing detected topics, allowing selection, and then triggering quiz generation. This involves changes to types, services and views. Specifically I'm going to add `detectTopics` to `geminiService.ts` and modify `generateQuizFromText` to accept topic strings.


**Adjusting for Chapter Selection**

I've been refining the quiz generation process to accommodate chapter-specific quizzes. The goal is to allow users to select one or multiple chapters to base the quiz on. I'm modifying the `generateQuizFromText` function to accept an array of strings representing the selected chapters. I'm currently adding a new function, `discoverTopics`, to analyze the text and find logical chapters/sections, returning a JSON array of chapter titles. This will replace the previous focus topic approach, and I'm updating `types.ts` to include a `DiscoveredTopic` structure.


**Automating Chapter Selection**

I'm now thinking about how to automate chapter detection and allow for selective quiz generation. The plan is to enhance the existing flow in `QuizGenerator.tsx` to include automatic topic discovery via `discoverTopics` after text extraction. This introduces a checklist UI for users to choose from automatically detected chapters, or manually input a topic as a custom override. The `selectedAutoTopics` will constrain the quiz topics. I'll modify the "Generate Quiz" function to accept the selected topics and combine them into a string. The "Detecting Chapters..." spinner will provide feedback during this process.


I'm currently focused on how to make chapter detection a seamless background process. Instead of a button, automatic topic extraction now initiates upon file upload. This requires UI updates to accommodate topic display and selection, including handling potential loading states for larger files. My latest plan is to update the file handling to extract the text and trigger `discoverTopics`, then display the results for user selection. The new UI will have an added section for chapter selection with checkboxes.


**Revising Topic Focus**

I've been revising the `generateQuizFromText` prompt to enhance focus. I've added a `focusTopics` list and will update the prompt to explicitly instruct Gemini to focus only on the selected chapters.  I'm also making necessary code changes in `types.ts` to include `DiscoveredTopic`, in `geminiService.ts` to add `discoverTopics` and update `generateQuizFromText`, and in `QuizGenerator.tsx` to manage new state variables like `topics`, `selectedTopicIds`, and `isAnalyzingFile`.  I'm also moving `extractTextFromFile` from `handleGenerate` to `handleFileChange` for better workflow, which will trigger analysis and UI updates for topic selection.


**Coding Topic Selection**

I'm now writing the code for the topic selection feature. I've updated the file upload handler to call `discoverTopics` and store extracted text to avoid re-reading the PDF. The UI updates will include a checklist for topic selection. I am building the topic schema. I'm focusing on defining a `topicsSchema` within `geminiService.ts`, and I'm updating `QuizGenerator.tsx` to utilize the results of the auto-detection to build the list of chapters, so the user can then generate a quiz based on their selection.


**Structuring the Quiz UI**

I'm now diving into the implementation details of the topic selection UI. I'm focusing on integrating the `isAnalyzingFile` state to provide visual feedback during topic discovery, updating the `QuizGenerator` component with a ref to store the extracted text, and modifying the UI to display the topic list retrieved from the `discoverTopics` function.  I'm also preparing for the parameter adjustments for `generateQuizFromText` to include optional `autoTopics` for focused quiz generation, which will impact prompt construction. The UI's loading states will be improved, now that topics will load asynchronously.

I will add the **Auto-Topic Detection & Chapter-wise Quizzes** feature.

Here are the changes:

1.  **`types.ts`**: Add `DiscoveredTopic` interface.
2.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**:
    *   Add `discoverTopics` function to analyze the document structure using Gemini.
    *   Update `generateQuizFromText` to accept a list of selected chapters/topics and strictly constrain the quiz generation to those sections.
3.  **`views/QuizGenerator.tsx`**:
    *   Automatically extract text and detect topics upon file upload.
    *   Add a "Chapters & Topics" selection panel in the configuration area.
    *   Allow users to select/deselect specific chapters to focus the quiz.
    *   Optimize performance by caching the extracted text so it doesn't re-process on generation.

#6 AI Flashcards Generator (Feature 11)

What & why

Generate flashcards (Q/A or term/definition) from the document for quick revision and export to apps like Anki.

UX

Button: “Generate Flashcards” with options: number per chapter, format (Q/A, term-definition).

Export to Anki (APKG), CSV, or printable PDF.

Implementation

Extract key terms (NLP: RAKE, spaCy entity extraction) and generate questions/answers or simple front/back cards.

Use LLM to rephrase terms into cues and definitions into answers or create cloze deletions.

Export

Anki: generate CSV or APKG (APKG requires Anki libraries or server-side packaging). CSV with fields [Front, Back, Tags] is simplest.

Quality checks

Remove duplicates; ensure answers are concise. Offer “merge similar cards” option.

Complexity: Low → Medium

**Envisioning Feature Implementation**

I've been wrestling with the "AI Flashcards Generator" concept, trying to visualize its implementation within the app. The "Generate Flashcards" button seems like the core interaction point. Now I'm focusing on defining options for customization – number per chapter and format options for sure. Exporting to Anki looks like a solid starting point.


**Developing Core Functionality**

I'm now diving into the nitty-gritty of the implementation. I'm focusing on defining crucial interfaces within `types.ts`, specifically `Flashcard` and `FlashcardSet`. I'm also planning the `generateFlashcardsFromText` function within `geminiService.ts` to facilitate the LLM interaction. Additionally, I need to implement the file service functions to create CSV and PDF outputs, and then construct the `FlashcardGenerator` view. Routing will be set up in `App.tsx`, and the home screen will get a shiny new button.


**Detailing Implementation Steps**

I'm presently building out the components in `types.ts`, focusing on the `ViewState` enum. The `geminiService.ts` file needs a function to create flashcards from text via a prompt. I'm also working on `fileService.ts` to support CSV and PDF export options for flashcards. The structure of the `FlashcardGenerator` view is taking shape, and `Home.tsx` and `App.tsx` need corresponding adjustments. The flashcard prompt needs refinement to ensure optimal JSON output.


**Constructing File Formats**

I'm now detailing the file service methods. For CSV, it's a straightforward string build with "Term,Definition". PDF will be either grid or list based on visual appeal. I'm focusing on the code now, updating `types.ts` and `services` files. I'm planning to use existing components for UI, so it might mean a minor tweak of existing `Card` components, nothing major. Next I'll start building the flashcard generator.


**Refining History Integration**

I'm presently updating `types.ts` to accommodate flashcards within the `HistoryItem` type. I'm expanding the union type for `data` in `HistoryItem` to include `FlashcardSet`. I'm also preparing for the implementation of `storageService.ts`, considering the addition of flashcard sets to the app's history, even though the initial prompt didn't specify it. I'm carefully reviewing the existing codebase to ensure seamless integration.


**Mapping User Interactions**

Now I'm focusing on user interactions and how they connect with the code. I'm starting with `FlashcardGenerator.tsx`, which will handle uploads, configurations for card count and style (Q&A or Term/Def), and card display in a grid format with flip functionality. I'll implement bottom bar options for exporting to CSV (Anki), PDF, and saving. I'll also add a "Flashcards" option to `Home.tsx`. Finally, I am updating `storageService.ts` to accommodate the new `FlashcardSet` data type in the history.


**Structuring Components and Services**

I'm presently refactoring `geminiService.ts` to refine the prompt, requesting a question/term on the front and an answer/definition on the back for each card. In `FlashcardGenerator.tsx`, the user flow is becoming clear: file upload, "Generating...", then a flip-enabled grid of cards with export and save buttons. The CSV export in `fileService.ts` will follow a standard format.


**Planning Component Integration**

I'm now mapping out the components and services that need updates. First, I'll focus on modifying `types.ts` to accommodate the new `FlashcardSet` within the history item. Following that, I'll update `services/fileService.ts` for export options. Then, I'll get the LLM integration in `services/geminiService.ts` set up. Finally, I will focus on integrating all these changes within the views: `FlashcardGenerator.tsx`, `Home.tsx`, `App.tsx`, and `History.tsx`, while simultaneously reviewing how `storageService.ts` handles the added `FlashcardSet` data.


**Implementing Error Handling**

I'm now focusing on robust error handling, essential for LLM interactions. I'll integrate `useSWR` with the LLM call to manage loading, error states, and retries. I'll need to update the `FlashcardGenerator` to display errors clearly. I'm also planning to incorporate a retry mechanism to handle potential LLM hiccups. Ensuring a smooth user experience is paramount, even when external services face issues.

I will add the **AI Flashcards Generator** feature. This allows users to generate study flashcards from their documents, preview them, and export them to Anki (CSV) or PDF.

Here are the changes:
1.  **`types.ts`**: Add `Flashcard`, `FlashcardSet` types and update `ViewState` and `HistoryItem`.
2.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Add `generateFlashcardsFromText` function.
3.  **`services/fileService.ts`**: Add `generateFlashcardsPDF` and `downloadAnkiCSV` functions.
4.  **`views/FlashcardGenerator.tsx`**: Create the main view for uploading, configuring (Concept/Definition vs Q/A), previewing (flip animation), and exporting flashcards.
5.  **`views/Home.tsx`**: Add a new card for Flashcards.
6.  **`views/History.tsx`**: Update history to support viewing/downloading saved flashcard sets.
7.  **`App.tsx`**: Add routing for the new view.

# Updated files:

## types.ts

Added `Flashcard` types and updated state definitions.

payload.text:
#7 AI Notes Generator (Smart Notes) (Feature 12)

What & why

Create structured notes: condensed bullets, mindmaps, comparison tables, formula sheets.

UX

Options: “Condensed Notes”, “Mindmap”, “Comparison Table”, “Formula Sheet.”

Choose granularity (short / medium / long).

Implementation

Condensed notes: chunk + summarize + combine.

Mindmap: extract headings & subheadings, convert to hierarchical JSON for frontend mindmap rendering (use libraries like react-flow or vis.js).

Comparison tables: detect pairs of topics and instruct LLM to produce a table (columns: Feature/Topic/Pros/Cons).

Formula Sheet: parse LaTeX or mathematical expressions using regex + SymPy for normalization.

Output

Provide export as PDF / DOCX and a JSON representation to render interactive notes.

Complexity: Medium

**Defining the AI Feature**

I'm now fully immersed in the "AI Notes Generator" project. I've been giving serious thought to the core functionalities, particularly how the process can convert uploaded documents into easily digestible, structured notes. The user experience is paramount, and I'm keen to make the notes as useful and actionable as possible. It's a challenging but very promising task!


**Outlining Note Generation**

I'm currently fleshing out the specific note formats this generator will produce. The options I'm considering are a summary, a mindmap, comparison tables, and a dedicated formula sheet. For each, I'm defining the granularity levels from short to long. The architecture and its implementation are top of mind, with detailed plans for both the output format and the backend generation. I've begun planning updates to types and services.


**Structuring Note Generation**

I'm now focusing on the specific prompts to generate different note types. For condensed notes, I'm thinking of a structured format akin to a summary. For mindmaps, the prompt will generate a nested JSON structure. Markdown tables or a JSON array are in the works for tables, and for formula extraction, I'm defining a prompt to extract the key formulas and their definitions. I'm also planning the `generateNotesPDF` function to render the results into a PDF with various formatting options, especially for tables and potentially a textual mindmap.


**Designing New Features**

I've been working on UI improvements now. I'm focusing on the "Smart Notes" card for the home page and adding a feature to view saved notes in the history section. I'm building out routing for the notes generator in `App.tsx` as well. For the mindmap, I'll use a recursive CSS/SVG tree component, as `react-flow` is not feasible. I'll make the export/save buttons soon. My prompts are also getting a re-work.

**Verifying Implementation Steps**

I've decided to refine the implementation plan. I've broken down the necessary changes. Adding specific data types and making services work in tandem with new UI components is now my main focus. I'm exploring how to generate the right data into PDF, as well, checking existing functionality for re-use. I've begun to define the data structures for each note type which is critical. I'm taking a cautious approach to HTML generation to avoid potential issues, so I'll request JSON instead.


**Finalizing Note Structures**

Currently, I'm solidifying the data structures for mindmaps and tables. For mindmaps, it's a nested `MindMapNode` interface, and tables will use a structure with `headers` and `rows` as arrays. The `generateSmartNotes` service will produce JSON schemas for mindmaps and tables, and markdown strings for other types. A basic recursive React component will be used for the mindmap, and the notes view will have state for type selection and will use the logic to render the appropriate format. Now, it's time to build this all out.

I will implement the **AI Notes Generator (Smart Notes)** feature.

This includes:
1.  **Smart Note Generation**: Create Condensed Notes, Mindmaps (visual tree), Comparison Tables, or Formula Sheets from documents.
2.  **Visual Mindmap Renderer**: A custom component to visualize hierarchical data generated by the AI.
3.  **Structured Data Support**: Comparison tables are generated as structured JSON for clean HTML rendering before export.
4.  **Export Options**: Save generated notes to History or download as PDF.

Here are the changes:
1.  **`types.ts`**: Add `NoteType`, `SmartNote`, and `MindMapNode` definitions.
2.  **`services/geminiService.ts`**: Add `generateSmartNotes` with specific prompting strategies for Mindmaps (JSON), Tables (JSON), and Text (Markdown).
3.  **`components/MindMap.tsx`**: Create a new visual component to render the mindmap tree structure.
4.  **`views/NotesGenerator.tsx`**: Create the main view for configuring and generating notes.
5.  **`views/Home.tsx`**: Add the "Smart Notes" entry card.
6.  **`views/History.tsx`**: Support viewing saved Smart Notes.
7.  **`App.tsx`**: Add routing for the new view.

Added `generateSmartNotes` function to handle different note types and formats (JSON vs Text).

New view for the Smart Notes feature.

## views/Home.tsx

## views/History.tsx

Added support for Smart Notes in history.

## App.tsx

Added routing for the Smart Notes view.

1 — High-level feature list (what you asked for)

Local signup with username/email + password (user chooses unique username and password).

Local login (username/email + password) — server verifies credentials.

Social login: Google Sign-in and Facebook Login (OAuth 2.0 / OpenID Connect flows).

Email verification at signup (required before sensitive actions / saving to history).

Forgot-password flow: user requests reset → receives secure single-use time-limited link → resets password.

Optional: link social accounts to an existing local account (account linking).

Account management: change password, enable/disable 2FA/MFA, view devices/sessions, delete account.

Auditing & security: rate limits, account lockout after repeated failed logins, secure password storage, logging.

Export & privacy: GDPR/CCPA notices for storing user files and consent when using third-party LLMs / cloud services.

**Developing Authentication Framework**

I'm currently focused on integrating authentication and user management into the React application. Given the frontend-only structure, I'm exploring various options to securely manage users. My initial thoughts center on implementing a robust authentication layer, potentially using a third-party service or building a custom solution. The goal is to provide secure access while maintaining the application's simplicity. I'm taking the local storage for history into consideration.


**Defining User Authentication Flow**

My focus has shifted to defining the core user authentication flow within the application. I'm prioritizing local signup/login with username and password, alongside social logins from Google and Facebook, which seem like the most straightforward starting points. Email verification and a "forgot password" flow are also under consideration, ensuring a comprehensive user experience. Security is paramount, so I'm also thinking through essential features such as rate limiting to mitigate potential vulnerabilities.


**Analyzing Authentication Limitations**

My thought process is now focused on the feasibility of different authentication approaches within the given constraints. I've realized that a fully secure solution with a persistent database backend is impossible. Option A, a `localStorage`-based mock system, presents the most straightforward implementation, but it is inherently insecure. Option B, utilizing a BaaS like Firebase or Supabase, is a more standard and secure approach. However, setup requires API keys and configuration, making it unsuitable for the current context. Option C is always open for review.


**Clarifying Backend Requirements**

My understanding has evolved significantly. I now see the "backend API spec" mentioned as a roadmap for future iterations, not a strict requirement for immediate implementation. Consequently, I'm focusing on building a robust frontend authentication layer. This includes all the requested UI screens, with an abstracted "AuthService" using `localStorage` for now. This approach allows users to immediately test the authentication flow, while keeping the architecture flexible for later API integration. Simulating email verification and social login interactions also makes sense for now.


**Refining Authentication Implementation**

My current focus is on a structured implementation. I'm building an `AuthProvider` for global state management, defining a `User` interface, and creating `authService.ts` for all auth actions. I'll mock async delays and use `localStorage` for now. This approach allows a usable frontend experience with mock features. I'm also building UI components for Login, Signup, Forgot Password, and User Profile. Finally, the plan is to integrate it all into App.tsx to create a functional system.


**Revising History Storage**

OAuth best practice: Use the provider's recommended libraries and OAuth2/OIDC flows (authorization code flow with PKCE for web apps). Follow Google and Facebook docs. 
Google for Developers
+1

Secure forgot-password flow: single-use, crypto-safe token, short expiry (e.g., 1 hour), delivered only to the account email, with rate-limiting and logging. Follow OWASP guidance.

4 — Database model (simplified)
users (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  username TEXT UNIQUE,
  email TEXT UNIQUE NOT NULL,
  email_verified BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  password_hash TEXT,             -- null if only social auth
  password_salt TEXT,             -- if using custom salting (Argon2 libs often include salt inside hash)
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  updated_at TIMESTAMP,
  is_disabled BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE
);

oauth_accounts (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id) ON DELETE CASCADE,
  provider TEXT,                  -- 'google' or 'facebook'
  provider_user_id TEXT,          -- id from provider
  provider_email TEXT,
  access_token TEXT (optional, encrypted),
  refresh_token TEXT (optional, encrypted),
  created_at TIMESTAMP
);

password_reset_tokens (
  token_hash TEXT PRIMARY KEY,    -- store hash of token for safety
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id),
  expires_at TIMESTAMP,
  used BOOLEAN DEFAULT FALSE,
  created_at TIMESTAMP
);

sessions or refresh_tokens (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id),
  refresh_token_hash TEXT,
  expires_at TIMESTAMP,
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  last_used_at TIMESTAMP,
  user_agent TEXT,
  ip_address TEXT
);

6 — Google Sign-In & Facebook Login: Step-by-step (practical)
Google (high level)

Create OAuth credentials in Google Cloud Console → OAuth 2.0 Client IDs → provide authorized redirect URIs (your https://yourdomain.com/api/auth/oauth/google/callback). 
Google Help
+1

Use authorization code flow (server side). For single-page apps, use Authorization Code Flow with PKCE.

Frontend: redirect user to Google authorization URL or use the official Google Identity library. On callback, get code.

Backend: exchange code for tokens (access_token, id_token). Verify the id_token (JWT) signature and extract user email & Google sub (user id).

If email exists in DB, either link provider to that user or log them in. If not, create new user (mark email_verified true if Google verified it).

Optionally save provider_user_id and refresh token (encrypted) to support re-login/refresh.

Docs: Google Sign-In & OAuth 2.0. 
Google for Developers
+1

Facebook (high level)

Create an app on Facebook for Developers → configure OAuth redirect URIs (callback). 
Facebook Developers

Use OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow. Request scopes email, public_profile (request only required scopes).

Exchange code for access token on backend. Use the token to call Facebook Graph API /me?fields=id,name,email to fetch user profile.

7 — Signup & Login flows (UX + server logic)
Signup (local)

Frontend: form — username, email, password, confirm password, TOS checkbox.

Client: check password strength (meter) and validate email format.

Server:

Validate uniqueness of username & email.

Hash password using Argon2id (or bcrypt) with a reasonably high work factor.

Save user with email_verified = false.

Issue email verification token (single-use, short expiry e.g., 24h), send link by email.

On clicking link, verify token and set email_verified = true.

Login (local)

Frontend: username or email + password.

Server:

Find user by username/email.

If is_disabled or email_verified required, block accordingly.

Compare password using the same hashing function (Argon2 verify).

On success: create session (cookie-based session with HttpOnly secure cookie) or return short-lived access token + refresh token.

On repeated failures: increase backoff, show generic error message, after N failures temporarily lock or require captcha.

Forgot password

Frontend: “Forgot password?” → enter email → submit.

Server:

Generate cryptographically secure random token (e.g., 32 bytes). Compute token_hash = HMAC_SHA256(token, server_pepper) or hash with bcrypt/Argon2 and store token_hash.

Save token record with expiry (e.g., 1 hour) and mark unused.

Send an email to the user with a link: https://yourdomain.com/reset-password?token=<token> (token in URL).

When user opens reset page and submits new password, server finds token_hash by hashing the provided token the same way and checks expiry and used=false. If valid, update user password (hash new password), mark token used, invalidate existing sessions/refresh tokens.

Inform user of success.

See OWASP Forgot Password Cheat Sheet for detailed guidance. 
OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Forgot_Password_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

**Defining Frontend Authentication**

I'm currently focused on defining the specific Signup and Login flows for the frontend. I need to integrate best practices like email verification, password strength checks, and secure password reset mechanisms into the user experience. I'm prioritizing seamless integration while ensuring robust security.

The `signup` method will generate a token, store its hash, and mark the user as unverified. I'm building out a `verify` method to validate tokens and update user status to verified. The `login` method needs updating to check verification status. I'll simulate "sending" verification emails via console logs. My goal is to make these functions match realistic interactions.


**Adding email verification logic**

I'm now integrating email verification into my `authService`. I've added a method to verify a user's email based on a token, ensuring proper hashing. During login, a new check now prevents access until the email is verified, with a tailored error message. I'm also ensuring token hashing for password reset and invalidating sessions after reset to enhance security. I'm building out the views to handle the complete verification cycle.

I've also implemented checks to prevent login for unverified users and developed the `resetPassword` flow. The new `views/Auth.tsx` is being refined to include password matching, TOS validation, and a `verify` mode UI.

8 — Password hashing & token storage (concrete)

Password hashing: use Argon2id (recommended) via libs:

Node.js: argon2 package.

Python: argon2-cffi.

If Argon2 unavailable, use bcrypt with cost ≥ 12.

Reset token: generate with crypto.randomBytes(32).toString('hex'). Store only a hash of the token (e.g., HMAC-SHA256 with server-side pepper or Argon2 hash) so if DB leaks tokens can’t be used. Set expires_at.

Refresh tokens: treat like reset tokens — store hash and implement rotation/invalidation on use.

OWASP Password Storage guidance explains details and recommends Argon2id. 
OWASP Cheat Sheet Series
https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Password_Storage_Cheat_Sheet.html?utm_source=chatgpt.com

**Considering Secure Mock-ups**

I'm exploring different ways to handle this asynchronous operation, likely involving `await` or `.then()` to ensure the correct data type is passed.

This will fix the type mismatch when setting the user state. I'm focusing on ensuring the User data is available before referencing its properties. This should resolve both the type mismatch error and the missing 'id' property.

11 — Account linking (social & local)

If user signs up with Google and later tries to sign up with same email via Facebook, detect same email and:

Option A: offer to link providers after email verification (recommended).

Option B: prompt to login using original method.

Provide UI under Account Settings: “Linked accounts” with connect/disconnect buttons.

**Pinpointing Account Conflicts**

I'm focusing now on detecting instances where users attempt to link accounts using the same email address across different providers. My main concern is to accurately pinpoint these conflicts early in the process. I believe that early detection is key to a smooth and user-friendly experience.


**Evaluating Login Strategies**

I'm now exploring the optimal user interface for account linking. I'm choosing between automatically merging accounts with the same email or requiring a manual login. Initially, I'll allow forcing a specific email for testing, or simply link if the email exists, and create if it doesn't. I'm exploring options, such as standardized mocks and adding a prompt in the Auth view during development, for more straightforward testing of account linking conflicts.

I'm focusing on the logic: searching the user database, checking linked providers, and implementing automatic linking (Option A) if no match is found. This refactor aims to handle all linking scenarios efficiently. I'm also preparing to update `Profile.tsx` for proper state reflection of linked accounts.  Finally, I'm adjusting mock email generation to be deterministic for consistent and reliable testing.

**Integrating Email Verification**

I've updated `socialLogin` in `authService.ts` to search for users by email and handle linking appropriately.  I'm also working on modifying the `Auth.tsx` view, by introducing a prompt that allows the user to simulate an email returned by the social provider. **Finalizing Disconnect Logic**

I'm now focused on the final aspects of account linking. I've successfully ensured the `Linked Accounts` UI prevents a user from disconnecting their only login method if no password is set. My current task is to implement the safeguard within `Profile.tsx`, specifically the check: `if (!user.password_hash && linkedAccounts.length === 1)` to prevent such disconnection.  I've already updated the `socialLogin` signature and the `Auth.tsx` view for simulation, and I'm confident I can make this final adjustment efficiently.


**Finalizing Linking Scenarios**

I've just finished the last details of the account linking flow, refining `Profile.tsx` to handle all possible states. This update allows simulating "Same Email" scenarios (e.g., signing in with Facebook using an email already registered via Google). It implements the **"Option A"** strategy: automatically detecting the existing user by email and linking the new provider to that account.

I will also add a safety check in the **Profile** view to prevent users from disconnecting a social provider if it is their *only* way to sign in (i.e., no password set and no other linked accounts).

#9 Export Options (Feature 15)
Where to put the Export / Download controls (UI locations)

Add export options in these places so users can download whatever they need from anywhere in the app:

Quiz Result Page (Primary)

Location: top-right of the results/header bar or sticky bottom-right floating button.

Why: Users usually want to download after they finish a quiz.

Behavior: “Download” button → opens a small modal or dropdown with format choices and toggles (Include answers? Include explanations?). Default: PDF.

Quiz Editor / Preview (Before Attempt)

Location: near the Save / Generate buttons (bottom-left / bottom-right).

Why: Teachers may want to export before giving quiz to students.

Behavior: Allow export of “Test copy” or “Answer key included”.

Summary Page

Location: top-left or top-right of the summary view (you requested left for summary earlier).

Why: Users often want to download summaries for reading offline.

Behavior: Simple format chooser (PDF / DOCX / TXT). Default: PDF.

History Page (Bulk Export)

Location: in each history item (item-level actions) and a global “Export selected” button for bulk export.

Why: Allow exporting multiple saved quizzes or summaries at once (ZIP).

Behavior: Checkbox select → “Export selected” → choose format (ZIP containing chosen formats).

Flashcards / Anki Export UI

Location: in Flashcards view: “Export to Anki / CSV” button.

Why: Flashcard users often want Anki imports.

Account Settings / Tools (Advanced Exports)

Location: Settings → Data Export (full account data: quizzes, summaries in JSON/ZIP)

Why: For GDPR-like data export or full backups.

3 — Client-side vs Server-side — how to choose

Client-side exports (fast, private) — do these in the browser when output is small:

PDF (jsPDF) for simple layouts, CSV, JSON, TXT, basic Anki CSV.

Pros: privacy (no server upload), immediate.

Cons: limited formatting, heavy exports may hang the browser.

Server-side exports (robust, feature-rich) — use server when:

You need complex formats (DOCX templates, PPTX slides, APKG for Anki, large PDFs with images), or heavy processing (rendering many pages), or batch exports.

Use server libs: python-docx, python-pptx, WeasyPrint/wkhtmltopdf, node docx-templater, libreoffice conversions, AnkiAPKG builder.

Pros: better formatting, can queue and notify, safer for big jobs.

Cons: server cost, must handle privacy and storage.

Rule of thumb: small/simple → client; complex/large/bulk → server.

I will integrate the server-side logic conceptually into `services/fileService.ts` to demonstrate the architecture for "Heavy Exports" (PPTX/DOCX) as requested, while keeping the existing "Client-Side" implementation for PDF/CSV active as the primary method. This aligns with the "Hybrid" approach (Client for simple, Server for complex).

Since I cannot deploy a real Python backend in this environment, I will:
1.  **Update `services/fileService.ts`**: Add placeholder functions for `generatePPTX` and `generateDOCX` that simulate a server request (latency + success response) and fallback to a client-side alert explaining the server requirement.

POST /api/export/prepare
Body: { type: "quiz"|"summary", id: "<quizId|summaryId>", format: "pdf|docx|csv|pptx|apkg|json|txt", options: {...} }
Response (sync small): returns file blob or presigned URL.

6 — Quick implementation hints & recommended libraries

Client-side (JS)

PDF: jsPDF (good for text-heavy, simple layout). Use html2canvas for HTML-to-PDF if you need to render styled content.

CSV: create CSV string and use Blob + URL.createObjectURL() to download.

JSON / TXT: simple blob downloads.

Server-side (Python / Node)

PDF (server): WeasyPrint, wkhtmltopdf, or ReportLab for fine control.

DOCX: python-docx (Python) or docx-templater (Node).

PPTX: python-pptx (Python).

Anki APKG: use genanki (Python) to build APKG files programmatically. Or export Anki-compatible CSV for import.

ZIP generation: zipfile (Python) or archiver / adm-zip (Node).

Storage: store generated files on server or upload to object storage (S3) and return signed download link.
