Default export behavior (full implementation guide)

Below is a complete, practical, developer-ready specification for the four default behaviors you asked about. It covers what each behavior does, why it helps, exact UI/UX, backend architecture and endpoints, data model, job queue & processing flow, notifications, security & privacy, retention & audit, error handling and monitoring, and sample code/pseudocode you can implement immediately.

1 — Behavior: Default Download = PDF without explanations (toggle visible)
What it does

When the user clicks Download on the Quiz Result page, the export modal opens. The default selected format is PDF. The export option Include explanations is OFF by default (toggle visible so user can enable it).

Why this helps

Prevents accidental sharing of answers (teachers/students use blank quizzes).

Keeps default file size smaller and faster to generate.

Gives clear control to users (explicit consent to include answers/explanations).

UI / UX details

Location: Quiz Result page → top-right button or sticky bottom-right floating button labeled Download.

Modal content:

Title: “Download Quiz”

Large format selector (radio/tile): PDF (default), DOCX, CSV, PPTX, Anki, JSON, TXT.

Toggles (visible):

 Include answers (default: OFF)

 Include explanations (default: OFF)

 Include my responses (default: OFF) — for history exports

Advanced (collapse):

Page size/orientation, include page citations, watermark options, filename field.

Buttons: Generate / Download (primary), Cancel.

Tooltip text next to toggles: “Include explanations: show explanation text under each question in the exported file.”

Small help text: “Default is OFF to prevent accidental sharing of answers.”

Implementation notes (frontend)

Store the toggle state in UI; pass options to export endpoint.

Remember user preference optionally in profile for next time (e.g., teachers prefer Include answers ON).

For large exports (PPTX/DOCX or bulk), submit server job & notify when ready
What it does

Large or complex export requests are processed asynchronously on the server. The frontend returns a jobId and shows a progress UI. When the export completes, the user gets an in-app notification and/or email with a secure download link.

Why this helps

Prevents browser hangs and timeouts during heavy processing.

Allows queueing, retry, and controlled resource usage.

Enables progress feedback and background completion notifications.

Architecture & components

Job queue: Redis-backed queue (Bull, BullMQ for Node; RQ, Celery for Python).

Worker processes: dedicated workers that consume jobs and create export files.

Object storage: S3 (AWS) or S3-compatible (MinIO) for storing generated files; use signed URLs for downloads.

Database: store job metadata and export records.

Notification service: in-app notifications (websocket/push) and emails (SendGrid/SES/Mailgun).

Expiration policy: generated files expire after configurable time (e.g., 7 days).

Flow (sequence)

Frontend posts export request to /api/export/prepare with { itemId, type: 'quiz'|'summary', format: 'pptx'|'docx'|'pdf'..., options }.

Backend validates request and user permissions. If estimated size/format is “large” (configurable rule), create job in queue and return { jobId, status: 'queued' }.

Worker picks up job, generates file (using python-pptx, python-docx, wkhtmltopdf, genanki, etc.), uploads file to S3, writes export record with status: ready and file_url (signed or object key).

Backend sends notification to user (websocket or push) and email with download link.

Frontend polls /api/export/status/:jobId or listens on websocket for status updates.

User clicks link and downloads file. File remains available until expires_at.

API Endpoints (suggested)

POST /api/export/prepare
Body: { sourceType: "quiz"|"summary", sourceId, format, options }
Response: { jobId, estimatedTimeSec?, status: "queued" }

GET /api/export/status/:jobId → { jobId, status: "queued"|"processing"|"ready"|"failed", progressPercent, fileUrl?, error? }

GET /api/export/download/:exportId → redirects to signed S3 URL or streams file (requires auth)

GET /api/export/list?userId= → list of recent exports

Worker considerations

Use time & memory limits for workers.

Use temporary local disk to assemble files; clean up after upload.

For heavy exports, chunk work (generate slides in parts) to avoid memory spikes.

Implementation notes (server)

Tag formats requiring server jobs: PPTX, DOCX (with images + fonts), APKG, bulk ZIP exports, exports > N pages/questions.

Use a “cost heuristic” to decide synchronous vs async: cost = questions * averageSizePerQuestion(format). If cost > threshold → async.

Save export log in user account (allow re-download within limited time)
What it does

Each generated export is recorded in the database with metadata and a link to the exported file. Users can re-download their recent exports from an Exports section in their History for a limited time (e.g., 7 days).

Why this helps

Convenience: no need to regenerate heavy files.

Auditability: user and admins can track exports, formats, options, dates.

Cost & performance: avoid repeated expensive exports.

Data model

Add an exports table (SQL example):

CREATE TABLE exports (
  id UUID PRIMARY KEY,
  user_id UUID REFERENCES users(id),
  source_type TEXT,         -- 'quiz' or 'summary'
  source_id UUID,
  format TEXT,              -- 'pdf','docx','pptx','csv','apkg','json','txt'
  options JSONB,            -- include answers/explanations etc.
  filename TEXT,
  storage_key TEXT,         -- S3 object key
  file_size BIGINT,
  status TEXT,              -- 'ready','failed','processing'
  created_at TIMESTAMP,
  expires_at TIMESTAMP,
  downloads_count INT DEFAULT 0
);

UX for export log

History > Exports: listing with columns: File name, Format, Created At, Expires In, Actions [Download, Delete].

Show preview icon and small summary of options (Include answers?).

Allow bulk delete, re-generate (regenerate creates new export job), and re-download.

Retention policy

Default retention: 7 days (configurable).

On expiry: worker deletes object from S3 and updates status=expired.

Optionally allow premium users longer retention (e.g., 30 days).

Security & access control

Ensure only owner (user_id) or admin can download. Signed links must be short-lived (e.g., 15 minutes).

Track downloads_count and last_downloaded_at.

Full technical design (putting all four together)
Frontend flow (detailed)

User finishes quiz → clicks Download.

UI opens Export Modal:

Default selected format = PDF

Toggles: Include answers = OFF, Include explanations = OFF

Format tiles display a small Sample link for each format.

User chooses format & options.

If chosen format is “client-capable” (CSV, JSON, small PDF), the frontend calls POST /api/export/prepare and requests synchronous generation; server responds with a signed URL or returns file blob.

If chosen format is “server-only” (PPTX/DOCX or huge PDF) or heuristics say job is large, frontend receives jobId. Display “Preparing… you’ll be notified”.

App shows sample link; user can download sample immediately.

Worker generates file; upon completion backend:

Uploads file to S3 (storage_key)

Creates exports DB row with status = ready, expires_at = now + retention

Sends websocket notification and email.

User downloads file via GET /api/export/download/:exportId which returns signed URL or streams the file.

Export is logged; user can later re-download from History > Exports until expiry.

Backend components & responsibilities

API server: validates requests, initiates jobs, serves small exports, handles sync vs async logic.

Queue/Worker: processes heavy exports, uses libraries to build files, uploads to storage.

Storage: S3 for files, DB for metadata.

Notifications: email and websocket push.

CDN: hosts samples.

Cost & scaling considerations

Heavy exports (PPTX with images, APKG) can be CPU & memory intensive — schedule them on dedicated worker instances.

Use ephemeral storage and delete temp files.

Use signed URLs and short-lived storage lifetime to reduce storage costs.

Security & privacy checklist for exports

Use HTTPS everywhere.

Authenticate requests for downloads (signed URLs expire; verify user or token).

Don’t store PII in filenames. Avoid putting sensitive user data in public object keys.

Optionally watermark exported files with Generated for <username> on <date> to discourage sharing.

For documents that were uploaded by user and processed by LLMs, display consent notice before exporting to third-party integrations.

Only allow exports for authorized users (the owner or allowed collaborators).

Error handling & UX recovery

If job fails: mark export record failed, send notification with error summary, and provide “Retry” button.

If storage upload fails: retry worker upload (with exponential backoff) or fallback to alternative storage.

If user clicks Download and signed URL expired: show friendly message “File expired — regenerate” with one-click regenerate.

Provide progress percent and estimated time for exports in status API.

Monitoring & observability

Metrics to capture:

Number of exports / format distribution

Average export generation time per format

Worker job failure rate

Storage usage & retention expiry rate

Alerts on worker errors > threshold.

Track job latency spikes to auto-scale workers.

Testing checklist

Unit tests for export API and options parsing.

E2E test for:

Small client-side export (CSV, small PDF)

Async job flow: prepare → process → upload → notify → download

Security: verify only owner can download

Retention: file expiry and regenerate flow

Performance test generating large PPTX with many images

Localization tests

3.  **Update `services/fileService.ts`**: Refactor `downloadQuizPDF` to use **`html2canvas`** as the primary capture method. This aligns with the "Puppeteer fallback" strategy: instead of rebuilding the PDF text-by-text (which caused the garbled output and layout issues), we snapshot the *exact* rendered DOM that the user sees, guaranteeing WYSIWYG fidelity.

This comprehensive fix addresses both the "garbled text" (by using the browser's rendering engine via canvas) and the "incorrect mode" (by applying the CSS classes before capture).

I've integrated `html2canvas` to capture each quiz card, added pagination logic for longer quizzes, and handled the asynchronous nature of the image generation. Now, the `downloadQuizPDF` function is fully operational and the generated PDF faithfully mirrors the visual layout of the quiz results.


**Crafting DOM Capture**

I'm now fully immersed in the `html2canvas` implementation. I'm building out the DOM capture for each quiz card, ensuring the PDF presents the intended visual fidelity. I am taking steps to integrate the new code, aiming to have it render the PDF as per the user's intent. My primary focus is on the visual presentation of each card and how to best render it on the PDF.


**Simplifying Download Logic**

I'm now streamlining the download process based on the "what you see is what you get" principle. I've realized that the current logic tried to generate extra quiz versions, which isn't the primary goal. My focus is now on capturing the exact visible result view, which aligns perfectly with using `html2canvas` for precise visual fidelity. I've noted that `handleExport` already provides the necessary view (`result`) after submission, so a direct DOM capture is appropriate. I'm updating `QuizGenerator.tsx` and `fileService.ts` to reflect these changes.


**Implementing Global Declaration**

I'm now incorporating `html2canvas` into the global interface to ensure proper type checking. This addition is essential for resolving any type-related issues when integrating the library. I am focusing on this step.

I will implement a visual "What You See Is What You Get" (WYSIWYG) PDF export using `html2canvas`. This ensures the downloaded PDF looks exactly like the interactive quiz on the screen (including colors, layout, and cards) rather than a plain text document.

Here are the changes:
1.  **`index.html`**: Add the `html2canvas` library to enable capturing the screen.
2.  **`views/QuizGenerator.tsx`**: Add a specific class `quiz-card` to the question cards so the exporter can identify exactly what to capture.
3.  **`services/fileService.ts`**: Completely rewrite `downloadQuizPDF` to capture the visual elements of the quiz and place them into the PDF as high-quality images, preserving the "Total Quiz" look.

#10 Question Timer (Feature 16)

What & why

Add per-question timer or global quiz timer for realistic exam practice.

UX

Toggle timer on/off. Set timer per question (e.g., 30s) or for whole quiz. Show progress bar & audible beep when time's up. Put this at t Quiz configuration in create new quiz. Put optional things also here only which i mentioned below in this.


Implementation

Frontend timer (JS) that either auto-selects “no answer” or locks in current selection when time ends.

Prevent cheating by disabling revisit in exam mode (optional).

Edge cases

Network interruptions: save answer states locally and sync when online.

Complexity: Low

I will implement the **Question Timer (Feature 16)** to enhance the quiz-taking experience.

Here is the plan:
1.  **Update `types.ts`**: Add timer configuration properties (`timerMode`, `timeLimit`) to the `Quiz` interface.
2.  **Update `views/QuizGenerator.tsx`**:
    *   Add UI controls in the "Quiz Configuration" step to toggle the timer (Per Question / Total / Off) and set the duration.
    *   Implement the timer logic during the "Taking Quiz" phase:
        *   Display a countdown bar/clock.
        *   Auto-advance (for per-question) or auto-submit (for total) when time runs out.
        *   Add visual warnings (red color) when time is low.

#13 Multi-language Support (Feature 20)

Support generating MCQs and summaries in multiple languages (English, Telugu, Hindi, etc.) — broadens user base.

UX

Language selector on Home and generation screens. Option to auto-detect source language.

Implementation

Translation vs Native generation:

Prefer generating directly in target language using LLM prompts that instruct the model to produce the output in that language.

Optionally: extract content in source language → use translation model → generate questions in target language.

Use multilingual models (OpenAI, Google, or LLMs supporting requested languages). For on-device TTS/STT choose services with multi-language coverage.

Challenges

Quality varies by language; test subject-specific terms for correctness.

what are the features i can ensure in this application to better  the user usage.

Here are several impactful features you could add to improve the application for users, categorized by impact:

### 1. Learning & Engagement (High Impact)
*   **Spaced Repetition System (SRS) for Flashcards:** Instead of just flipping cards, implement an algorithm (like SM-2 used in Anki) that schedules reviews based on how well the user knows a card (Again, Hard, Good, Easy). This significantly boosts long-term retention.
*   **Gamification:**
    *   **Streaks:** Track consecutive days of study.
    *   **Badges/Achievements:** Awards for "First Quiz Aced", "100 Flashcards Reviewed", "Summary Master".
    *   **Leaderboards:** Optional global or friend-based rankings for quiz scores.
*   **Interactive Study Modes:**
    *   **Match Mode:** Drag and drop terms to definitions (for Flashcards).
    *   **Fill-in-the-Blanks:** Generate quizzes where key terms are removed from summary sentences.

### 2. Content & Generation Quality (Medium Impact)
*   **Multi-File Context:** Allow users to upload *multiple* PDFs (e.g., "Chapter 1" and "Chapter 2") and generate a combined quiz or summary that synthesizes information across all documents.
*   **Customizable Prompts:** For advanced users, allow editing the system instructions sent to Gemini (e.g., "Focus on dates and names" or "Explain like I'm 5").
*   **Image Analysis (OCR):** Currently, the app likely extracts text. enhancing it to analyze charts, graphs, and diagrams within PDFs to generate questions about visual data would be a killer feature for STEM subjects.
*   **Audio/Podcast Generation:** Convert the generated summary into a conversational audio file (like a podcast episode) using Gemini's multi-modal capabilities or a TTS service, so users can "listen" to their notes while commuting.

### 3. User Experience & Accessibility (Medium Impact)
*   **Dark Mode:** A full system-wide dark theme for late-night study sessions (reduces eye strain).
*   **Progress Analytics Dashboard:** A visual graph showing quiz scores over time per subject/topic. Help users identify their weak areas (e.g., "You consistently score low on 'History' but high on 'Science'").
*   **Collaborative Study Groups:** Allow users to share a "Classroom" code where they can post quizzes they generated for others to take.
*   **Offline Support (PWA):** Make the app a Progressive Web App so it can be installed on mobile phones and work offline (accessing previously generated/saved content).

### 4. Monetization/Scale (Business Impact)
*   **Freemium Limits:** Limit guest users to 3 quizzes/day and offer a "Pro" tier for unlimited generation and advanced models (Gemini 1.5 Pro vs Flash).
*   **Cloud Sync:** Move from `localStorage` to a real backend (Firebase/Supabase) so user data syncs across their phone and laptop.

### Quickest Wins (Do these first):
1.  **Dark Mode:** Easy to implement with Tailwind.
2.  **Progress Analytics:** You already save quiz scores; just visualize them with a simple line chart library.
3.  **Shareable Links:** Allow users to export a quiz as a JSON file or a unique URL (if you add a backend) to send to a friend.
